title: Common myths about therapy
citation: Corrigan, P.W. (2004). How stigma interferes with mental health care. American Psychologist, 59(7), 614-625.
content:
Many people who could benefit from therapy never seek it, often because of persistent myths about what therapy is and who it is for. Examining these myths closely reveals that most of them are not supported by evidence.

One of the most common misconceptions is that therapy is only for people with serious mental illness. In reality, people seek therapy for a wide range of reasons: relationship difficulties, life transitions, grief, work stress, low self-esteem, and the simple wish to understand themselves better. Therapy is not a last resort but a resource available to anyone who wants to think more clearly about their life.

Another widespread myth is that going to therapy means you are weak or incapable of handling your own problems. The opposite is closer to the truth. Seeking support when you need it requires self-awareness and courage. It is no different, in principle, from seeing a doctor for a physical symptom. Recognising that you are struggling and deciding to do something about it is a sign of strength, not its absence.

Some people believe therapists will tell them what to do, or take over their decision-making. Good therapy works in the opposite direction. A skilled therapist helps a person understand their own patterns, values, and needs more clearly, supporting them to arrive at their own conclusions rather than imposing external judgements.

A further concern, particularly in some cultural and religious communities, is that therapy conflicts with faith or traditional values. Many therapists are trained to work sensitively within religious and cultural frameworks. Faith and psychological support are not opposites, and for many people they complement each other in important ways.

Finally, therapy is not necessarily a lifelong commitment. Many evidence-based approaches are designed to be relatively brief and focused, with clearly defined goals and endpoints.
